name: Step 4 — On-Prem Qwen LLM + All MCP Servers

options.systemMessage:
=# Network Agent

## Role

You are a concise, factual network engineering assistant with access to these MCP Servers:

* ThousandEyes (User Endpoint Analytics)
* Meraki (Campus Network Management Platform)
* Nexus (Data Center switching management)
* Intersight (Infrastructure device management for Cisco UCS systems)
* ITSM (IT Service Management for change control and incident tracking)

## Behavior

* On the first message only, greet the user and say you can assist with network operations.
* Never invent information. Only use content found in the tools.
* Be efficient. Gather only the data needed to answer the question. Do not make exploratory tool calls.
* Before making a tool call, check if the answer was already retrieved earlier in this conversation.
* When calling any ThousandEyes MCP tool, you MUST always include "aid": "369116" in every tool call. Never omit this parameter, even if not explicitly requested.
* When calling Meraki MCP tools, default to organization ID 3705899543372497758 and network ID L_3705899543372507602, unless otherwise specified.

## Answer Format

* Use bullet points or short paragraphs.
* Include a section **"Sources checked:"** listing the API calls used.

## Edge Cases

* If you find too little information, say so and suggest adding more data sources to the agent.
* If the user asks for opinions or predictions, stay neutral and summarize facts only.

## Date

Today is {{ $today }}

parameters.content:
## Step 6: On-Prem Qwen LLM + All MCP Servers

**What changed from Step 5:**
- ✅ Swapped LLM: Claude Haiku → Qwen3.5-9B (on-premise)
- ✅ Added: Nexus MCP Client (data center switching)
- ✅ Added: Intersight MCP Client (UCS compute management)
- ✅ Added: ITSM MCP Client (change + incident management)
- ✅ Updated: System prompt lists all 5 tools

**Why on-premise?**
Data sent to cloud LLMs (Claude, GPT) leaves your perimeter. For sensitive network operations data, running an LLM on-premises keeps all data inside your infrastructure. Qwen3.5-9B is small enough to run on a single GPU server.

**Tradeoffs:**
- Smaller context window than cloud models
- Slower response time (depends on GPU)
- No external data egress
- Lower cost at scale

**Example questions:**
- "What clients are on the Meraki network?"
- "Show Nexus switch VLAN configuration"
- "What is the firmware status of my UCS servers in Intersight?"
- "Are there any open incidents in ITSM?"
- "Is there a change request scheduled that could explain the current ThousandEyes alert?"

## LLM Model











The LLM will adopt the persona specified in the system prompt, interpret user messages, determine which MCP tools are needed to gather data, and provide the response.

Can be a cloud service (Claude, GPT, Gemini, etc) or hosted on-prem on a GPU running a local model.

**Step 6: Qwen3.5-9B is the active LLM.**
The Anthropic node is kept for reference — reconnect it to compare cloud vs. on-prem behavior.

## Memory











Allows agent to store the last few messages in memory to stay on topic. Larger memory allows for longer connected conversations but will consume more tokens.

**Note:** On-prem models have smaller context windows — keep memory window modest (10–20 messages) to avoid overflowing.

## ThousandEyes
MCP Client






The ThousandEyes MCP Server provides access to your enterprise's ThousandEyes data:

* **Core Monitoring**
  _Get details on tests, events, alerts, and outages_

* **Endpoint Monitoring and Network Path Analysis**
  _Get endpoint agent tests and metrics_

* **Advanced Analysis**
  _Get anomalies and metrics_

https://docs.thousandeyes.com/product-documentation/integration-guides/thousandeyes-mcp-server

**Selected tools enabled:**
- get_account_groups
- search_outages
- list_events / get_event
- list_endpoint_agents
- list_endpoint_agent_tests
- get_endpoint_agent_metrics

## Meraki
MCP Client






The Meraki MCP Server provides access to your enterprise's Meraki environment data:

The entire Meraki API of nearly 900 endpoints is available as tools.

This demo uses Selent-AI's open-sourced Meraki MCP wrapper:
https://github.com/selent-ai/selent-mcp

## Nexus
MCP Client











This MCP Server provides access to Nexus Dashboard — Cisco's data center network management platform.

Advertised tools:

* **Switch Configuration**
  _Retrieve current switch configuration including interfaces, VLANs, and routing_

* **VLAN Management**
  _Manage VLAN configuration, assignments, and trunk settings_

* **Network Topology**
  _View network topology, device connections, and link status_

## Intersight
MCP Client











This MCP Server provides access to Intersight — Cisco's compute management platform for UCS systems.

Advertised tools:

* **Device Inventory**
  _Retrieve current device inventory including servers, switches, and storage_

* **Firmware Status**
  _Check firmware versions and update status across all managed devices_

* **Hardware Monitoring**
  _Get hardware health status, temperatures, and performance metrics_

## ITSM
MCP Client











This MCP Server provides access to IT operations and service management.

Advertised tools:

* **Change Management**
  _Manage change requests, approvals, and implementation schedules_

* **Incident Management**
  _Track incident reports, status updates, and resolution progress_

* **IT Knowledge Base**
  _Access knowledge base articles, procedures, and documentation_
